concepts.title: Summation of series when the difference of the terms or difference of the differences are in Arithmetic Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Summation of Series

To find the summation of a series we follow 2 basic steps.

Step 1 : Find the ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term $\left( {{\text{T}}_{\text{n}}} \right)$

Step 2 : Find the sum using formula (for summation, using ${{\text{T}}_{\text{N}}}$ )

To find the ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term of a series we try to find out the differences between successive terms. If the difference is constant then they are in A.P and sum can be calculated by using a formula. But many times you will find different differences between successive terms.

For ex.

In such cases,

we find second order difference (i.e. we again find difference of differences obtain)

This process of finding the ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ order difference continues unless we get a constant difference.

Also, we can divide them into levels.

  1. $S=1,3,5,7\ldots \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Level 0 (typical AP with constant difference)
  2. $S^{\prime}=$ Level 1 (difference are in AP)
  3. $S=$ Level 2 (difference of differences are in AP)

So now we can generalize and find the results.

for ex. $\text{S}=1,3,6,10$

We know this is level 1.

& for level $1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\text{a}{{\text{r}}^{2}}+\text{br}+\text{c}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{1}}=\text{a}+\text{b}+\text{c}=1$ (a)

${{\text{T}}_{2}}=4\text{a}+2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}+\text{c}=3$ (b)

${{\text{T}}_{3}}=9\text{a}+3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ b}+\text{c}=6$ (c)

Using the following [(b) - (a) & (c) - (b), we get $3a+b=2\And 5a+b=3$

Subtracting we get $\text{a}=1/2$

Similarly we can find $b\And c$ now which comes out to $b=1/2,c=0$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{2}+\frac{\text{r}}{2}=\frac{\text{r}}{2}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)$

We can easily find the sum of this series now using formula for $\sum {{\text{n}}^{2}}\And \sum \text{n}$.

Let’s take another example, this time of level 2.

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ We }\!\!~\!\!\text{ have }\!\!~\!\!\text{ S}=1,2,5,11,21$

We know it is level 2, so

$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=a \mathrm{r}^3+\mathrm{br}^2+\mathrm{cr}+\mathrm{d}$

$\therefore$ $\mathrm{T}_1=1=\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}$

$\mathrm{T}_2=2=8 \mathrm{a}+4 \mathrm{~b}+2 \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}$

$\mathrm{T}_3=5=27 \mathrm{a}+9 \mathrm{~b}+3 \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}$

$\mathrm{T}_4=11=64 \mathrm{a}+16 \mathrm{~b}+4 \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{d}$

solving eqn. we get $a=1 / 6, b=0, c=-1 / 6, d=1$

$\therefore$ $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{r}^3}{6}-\frac{\mathrm{r}}{6}+1$

This is how we solve these kind of questions, now we will move to questions where differences are in G.P.

concepts.title: Summation of series when the difference of the terms or difference of the differences are in Geometric Progression
concepts.conceptBody:
Difference in G.P 

These are kind of questions in which differences successive terms is in GP instead of AP. There are 2 methods to solve these kind of problems.

Method 1 : It is the method of finding ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}$ term with the help of levels (formulas)

Level 0

(Simple GP)

Here the series is clearly in GP,

Level 1

Level 2

So,

Method 2 : 

This method involves the rearrangement of the given series in such a way that we get a simple GP series in between the given series.

Let us take an example to clear the concept.

We have, ${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=1+3+7+15+\ldots .+{{\text{T}}_{\text{n}}}$

now shift the values of ${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}$ & subtract it from ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}$ only.

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+3+7+15+\ldots . . \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}$

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=1+3+7+\ldots \cdot \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}$

$0$ $=\underbrace{1+2+4+8 \quad \ldots . .(\mathrm{n}-1) \text { terms }}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}$

now this is a general

GP with $r=2$

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\underbrace{1+[2+4+8+\ldots . .(\mathrm{n}-1) \text { terms }]}$

this becomes a simple GP for $\text{n}$ terms.

$\therefore$ $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1\left(2^{\mathrm{n}}-1\right)}{2-1}=2^{\mathrm{n}}-1$

or $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=2^{\mathrm{r}}-1$

$\therefore$ $\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(2^{\mathrm{r}}-1\right)=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} 2^{\mathrm{r}}-\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} 1$

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{2(1-2)^{\mathrm{n}}}{1-2} \mathrm{n}=2^{\mathrm{n}+1}-2-\mathrm{n}$

concepts.title: Some Special Series frequently used in solving problems
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Special Series 

(1) $\sum \text{n}=1+2+3+\ldots ..+\text{n}=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ (i.e. sum of first $\text{n}$ natural numbers)

This is an AP with $\text{a}=1$ and $\text{d}=1$

So, $\sum \text{n}=\frac{\text{n}}{2}\left( 2.1+\left( \text{n}-1 \right)1 \right)$

$\sum ~\text{n}=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}{2}$

(2) $\sum {{\text{n}}^{2}}={{1}^{2}}+{{2}^{2}}+{{3}^{2}}+\ldots +{{\text{n}}^{2}}=\frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)\left( 2\mathbf{n}+1 \right)}{6}$ 

Proof : $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(\text{x}+1)}^{3}}-{{\text{x}}^{3}}=3{{\text{x}}^{2}}+3\text{x}+1$

Putting $\text{x}=1,2,3,4,\ldots \text{n}$ then

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{2}^{3}}-{{1}^{3}}={{3.1}^{2}}+3.1+1 \\ {} & {{3}^{3}}-{{2}^{3}}={{3.2}^{2}}+3.2+1 \\ {} & {{4}^{3}}-{{3}^{3}}={{3.3}^{2}}+3.3+1 \\ {} & {{5}^{3}}-{{4}^{3}}={{3.4}^{2}}+3.4+1 \\ {} & ~{{(n+1)}^{3}}-{{n}^{3}}=3.{{n}^{2}}+3n+1 \\ \end{array}$

Adding all we get,

$(\mathrm{n}+1)^3-1^3=3\left(1^2+2^2+\ldots \ldots+\mathrm{n}^2\right)+3(1+2+\ldots \ldots .+\mathrm{n})+(1+1 \ldots . \mathrm{n}$ times )

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{n}^3+3 \mathrm{n}^2=3 \mathrm{n}=3 \sum \mathrm{n}^2=3 \sum \mathrm{n}+\mathrm{n}$

$\therefore$ $3 \sum n^2=n^3+3 n^2+3 n-3 \frac{n(n+1)}{2}-n$

$\Rightarrow$ $\sum \mathrm{n}^2=\frac{\mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6}$

(3) $\sum {{\text{n}}^{3}}={{1}^{3}}+{{2}^{3}}+{{3}^{3}}\ldots +{{\text{n}}^{3}}={{\left( \frac{\text{n}\left( \text{n}+1 \right)}{2} \right)}^{2}}$

Proof : $\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{(\text{x}+1)}^{4}}-{{\text{x}}^{4}}=4{{\text{x}}^{3}}+6{{\text{x}}^{2}}+4\text{x}+1$

Putting $\text{x}=1,2,3,4\ldots \ldots \text{n}$ then,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{2}^{4}}-{{1}^{4}}={{4.1}^{3}}+{{6.1}^{2}}+4.1+1 \\ {} & {{3}^{4}}-{{2}^{4}}={{4.2}^{3}}+{{6.2}^{2}}+4.2+1 \\ {} & {{4}^{4}}-{{3}^{4}}={{4.3}^{3}}+{{6.3}^{2}}+4.3+1 \\ {} & ~{{(\text{n}+1)}^{4}}-{{\text{n}}^{4}}=4.{{\text{n}}^{3}}+6.{{\text{n}}^{2}}+4.\text{n}+1 \\ \end{array}$

Adding all we get,

$(n+1)^4-1^4=4\left(1^3+2^3+\ldots . .+n^3\right)+6\left(1^2+2^2+\ldots \ldots .+n^2\right)+4(1+2+\ldots .+n)+$

$(1+1+\ldots \ldots+n$ times)

$\Rightarrow$ $\mathrm{n}^4+4 \mathrm{n}^3+6 \mathrm{n}^2+4 \mathrm{n}=4 \sum \mathrm{n}^3+6 \sum \mathrm{n}^2+4 \sum \mathrm{n}+\mathrm{n}$

$\Rightarrow$ $4 \sum \mathrm{n}^3+\frac{6 \cdot \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)}{6}+\frac{4 \cdot \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{n}+1)}{2}+\mathrm{n}$

$\Rightarrow$ $4 \sum n^3=n^4+4 n^3+6 n^2+4 n-n(n+1)(2 n+1)-2 n(n+1)-n$

$=\mathrm{n}^2(\mathrm{n}+1)^2$

$\sum n^3=\left\{\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right\}^2$

concepts.title: Summation of series where the nth term can be expressed as the difference of another two consecutive terms  and some solved problems based on it
concepts.conceptBody:
Summation of Series (Method-2) 

The following method works in the case when the ${{\text{n}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ term of a series can be expressed as the difference of two quantities.

i.e $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{T}_{r}}=f\left( r \right)-f\left( r-1 \right)$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\text{f}\left( \text{r} \right)-\text{f}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)$

Steps to be followed in such a case

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ find the general term $\left( {{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}} \right)$
  • Express the general term as the difference of two consecutive terms
  • In summation part (from 0/1 to $\text{n}$ ) (n) goes to the greater term & (1) goes to termwise smaller term Let us solve some questions to understand the rule.

Q) Find ${{S}_{n}}$, where ${{S}_{n}}=\frac{1}{1.2}+\frac{1}{2.3}+\frac{1}{3.4}\ldots (n$ terms $)$ 

Solution : 

Step 1 : find general term

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{\text{r}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)}$

Step 2 : expressing ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}$ as difference of 2 terms here methods of partial fraction can be used

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{\text{r}}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+1}$

Step 3: ${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\sum _{\text{r}=1}^{\text{n}}{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\sum _{\text{r}=1}^{\text{n}}\left( \frac{1}{\text{r}}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+1} \right)$

now the rule says that place $\text{n}$ in greater term (term wise not by value) which is $\text{r}+1$ here.

$\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}+1}=\left(1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}+1}\right)$

$=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}+1}$

$\therefore$ $S_n=\frac{n}{n+1}$

Q) Find ${{S}_{n}}$ where ${{S}_{n}}=\frac{1}{1.2.3}+\frac{1}{2.3.4}\ldots (n$ terms $)$ 

Solution : finding general term first

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{\text{r}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)\left( \text{r}+2 \right)}$

this can be rewritten as

$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{(\mathrm{r}+2)-\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{r}+1)(\mathrm{r}+2)}\right)$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{r}+1)}-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{r}+1)(\mathrm{r}+2)}\right)$

Note here that though in numerator we could have many options like $\left[ \left( r+1 \right)-r \right]$ etc. but we use the above one (i.e. $\left( \left( r+2 \right)-r \right)$ ) because after that we get terms which contains consecutive term wise terms (like $r\left( r+1 \right)\And \left( r+1 \right)\left( r+2 \right)$. The rule is valid for consecutive terms only.

here $\frac{1}{\left( r+1 \right)\left( r+2 \right)}$ is obtained by replacing $r$ by $r+1$ in $\frac{1}{r\left( r+1 \right)}$, which is what we require for rule to be valid.

Now, ${{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\sum _{\text{r}=1}^{\text{n}}{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\sum _{\text{r}=1}^{\text{n}}\frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{1}{\text{r}\left( \text{r}+1 \right)}-\frac{1}{\left( \text{r}+1 \right)\left( \text{r}+2 \right)} \right)$

Now applying step 3

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \underbrace{\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{r}+1)}-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{r}+1)(\mathrm{r}+2)}}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{(\mathrm{n}+1)(\mathrm{n}+2)}\right)$

Q) Find ${{S}_{n}}$, where $\frac{1}{1.3.5}+\frac{1}{3.5.7}+\ldots (n$ terms $)$ 

Solution : ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right)}$ 

again we will try to divide the term in 2 parts

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{4}\left[ \frac{\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right)-\left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)}{\left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right)} \right] \\ {} & {{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{4}\text{ }\!\!\{\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{\left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)}-\frac{1}{\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right)}\text{ }\!\!]\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

Now, we know that the second term can be achieved by replacing $r$ by $r+1$ in first term.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,\text{Tr}=\frac{1}{4}\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,\left( \frac{1}{\left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)}-\frac{1}{\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right)} \right.$

according to step 3

${{S}_{n}}=\frac{1}{4}\left( \frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{\left( 2n+3 \right)\left( 2n+3 \right)} \right)$

Q) Find ${{S}_{n}}$, where $\frac{1}{1.3}+\frac{1}{2.4}+\frac{1}{3.5}\ldots (n$ terms $)$ 

Solution : ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{\text{r}\left( \text{r}+2 \right)}$

$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{(\mathrm{r}+2)-\mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{r}(\mathrm{r}+2)}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{r}-\frac{1}{r+2}\right]$ (i)

now still we don't have term wise consecutive terms, so we will try to achieve that adding & subtracting $\frac{1}{\text{r}+1}$ in (i)

${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( \frac{1}{\text{r}}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+1} \right)+\left( \frac{1}{\text{r}+1}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+2} \right) \right]$

now the 2 block above are term wise consecutive respectively.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}} & ~=\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( \frac{1}{\text{r}}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+1} \right)+\left( \frac{1}{\text{r}+1}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+2} \right) \right] \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,\left( \underset{\text{r}}{\mathop{\underset{}{\mathop{\left. \frac{1}{\text{r}}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+1} \right)}}\,}}\,+\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,\left( \frac{1}{\text{r}+1}-\frac{1}{\text{r}+2} \right) \right] \right. \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( 1-\frac{1}{\text{n}+1} \right)+\left( \frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{\text{n}+2} \right) \right] \\ \end{array}$

(applying step 3)

Q) Find ${{S}_{n}}$, where ${{S}_{n}}=1.3.5+3.5.7+5.7.9+\ldots (n$ terms $)$ 

Solution : Here, ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right)$

Now here too we have to divide ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}$ into 2 terms.

Trick : Let a be the term before $\left( 2r-1 \right)$ i.e. $\left( 2r-3 \right)\And b$ be the term after $\left( 2r+3 \right)$ i.e. $\left( 2r+5 \right)$ multiply and divide by $\left( b-a \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{1}{8}\left[ \left( 2\text{r}+5 \right)-\left( 2\text{r}-3 \right) \right]\left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)\left( 2\text{r}+3 \right) \\ \end{array}$

Here the 2nd block can be achieved by replacing $r$ by $\left( r-1 \right)$ in the first block

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Ist block is term wise greater than 2nd block

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ We can now apply step 3

$\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}$

$=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{1}{8}[(2 \mathrm{r}+5)(2 \mathrm{r}+3)(2 \mathrm{r}+1)(2 \mathrm{r}-1)-(2 \mathrm{r}+3)(2 \mathrm{r}+1)(2 \mathrm{r}-1)(2 \mathrm{r}-3)]$

$=\frac{1}{8}[(2 \mathrm{n}+5)(2 \mathrm{n}+3)(2 \mathrm{n}+1)(2 \mathrm{n}-1)-(5)(3)(1)(-1)]$

$=\frac{1}{8}[(2 n+5)(2 n+3)(2 n+1)(2 n-1)+15]$

Q) Find ${{S}_{n}}$, where ${{S}_{n}}=\frac{1}{1.3}+\frac{1}{1.3.5}+\frac{1}{1.3.5.7}\ldots (n$ terms $)$ 

Solution : firstly, ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{\text{r}}{1.3.5\ldots \left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)}$

now we will try to break ${{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}$ into 2 term wise consecutive terms

$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{2 \mathrm{r}}{1.3 .5 \ldots(2 \mathrm{r}+1)}\right]$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{(2 \mathrm{r}+1)-1}{1.3 .5 \ldots(2 \mathrm{r}+1)}\right]$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{1.3 .5 \ldots(2 \mathrm{r}-1)}-\frac{1}{1.3 .5 \ldots(2 \mathrm{r}+1)}\right]$

$(\because $ Previous term of $\left( 2r+1 \right)$ is $\left( 2r-1 \right)$

Now we have term wise consecutive terms

Applying step 3

$\text{Sn}=\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,{{\text{T}}_{\text{r}}}=\underset{\text{r}=1}{\overset{\text{n}}{\mathop \sum }}\,\frac{1}{2}\left[ \frac{1}{1.3.5\ldots \left( 2\text{r}-1 \right)}-\frac{1}{1.3.5\ldots \left( 2\text{r}+1 \right)} \right]$

($\therefore $ The second term is term wise greater)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{S}}_{\text{n}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[ 1-\frac{1}{1.3.5\ldots \left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)} \right]$

examples.title: The sum of $(n+1)$ terms of $\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{1+2}+\frac{1}{1+2+3}+\ldots \ldots$ is
The sum of $(n-1)$ terms of $1+(1+3)+(1+3+5)+\ldots \ldots$ is

The sum to $n$ terms of the series $2^2+4^2+6^2+$\ldots\ldotsis
The sum of $n$ terms of the series whose $n^{\text {hth }}$ term is $n(n+1)$ is equal to
The sum of the series $3.6+4.7+5.8+\ldots \ldots \ldots$. uppto $(n-2)$ terms
The sum of the series $1.2 .3+2.3 .4+3.4 .5+\ldots \ldots .$. to $n$ terms is
The $n^{\text {th }}$ term of series $\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1+2}{2}+\frac{1+2+3}{3}+\ldots \ldots .$. will be
If the $n^{\text {th }}$ term of a series be $3+n(n-1)$, then the sum of $n$ terms of the series is
The sum to infinity of the following series $\frac{1}{1.2}+\frac{1}{2.3}+\frac{1}{3.4}+\ldots \ldots \ldots .$. shall be
The sum to $n$ terms of $(2 n-1)+2(2 n-3)+3(2 n-5)+\ldots . \cdot$ is
